macro_segments.title: The Debate Between Rav and Rabbi Yochanan on Replacement Goats and the Principle of Mitzvah B'Rishon

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Core Debate: Which Goat Do You Use When One Dies and a New Lottery Is Drawn
Rav's Position: Use the Original Goat Based on Mitzvah B'Rishon; the Original Pair Remains Usable
Rabbi Yochanan's Position: The Original Goat Becomes Nidcheh (Rejected) and Cannot Be Used
The Underlying Principle: Ba'alei Chayim Nidchim—Can a Living Animal Become Permanently Unusable
Tosafot's Question: Why Not Apply the Muvchar Mimenu Principle (Better Quality) to the Goats
Tosafot's Answer: The Muvchar Principle Applies Only to Things Within Human Experience, Not to Hidden Offerings
Philosophical Reflection: Aesthetics and Externals in the Temple Service as Concessions to Human Psychology

macro_segments.title: Proof from the Mishnah: When a Private Sin Offering Is Replaced, the Original One Dies

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Ruling: A Communal Sin Offering Does Not Die When Replaced, Unlike a Private One
Gemara Attempts to Prove Rav's Position via the Mishnah
Rabbi Abba's Teaching in Rav's Name: The Case of a Lost Offering and Its Replacement
When You Use the Replacement, the Original Dies; When You Use the Original, the Replacement Grazes
Distinction Between Replacement and Backup: Two Animals Separated for Backup Are Not the Same as Substitution
Rav's Explanation: The New Pair in the Goat Case Functions as Backup, Not Replacement, So the Original Doesn't Die
Gemara's Resolution: Since Mitzvah B'Rishon Suggests You Plan to Use the First Goat, It's Like Designated for Loss from the Start

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehudah's Disagreement and the Principle of Ba'alei Chayim Nidchim

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehudah's Position: Even a Communal Sin Offering Dies When Not Used
Clarification: Rabbi Yehudah Refers to the First Goat Dying, Not the Same Animal Twice
Rabbi Yehudah's Second Disagreement: Which Goat Is Rejected—He Reverses the Pairing Based on Ba'alei Chayim Nidchim
Two Debates Between Rav and Rabbi Yehudah vs. One Between Rav and Rabbi Yochanan
The Blood Spills Scenario: Proof of Ba'alei Chayim Nidchim According to Rabbi Yehudah
Rashi's Powerful Teaching: The Core Requirement Is Only the Lottery; Confession and Sending Off Are Not Ultimately Necessary
Why the Scapegoat Must Remain Alive: The Verse Demands It Stand Before God During the Atonement Process

macro_segments.title: The Question of Using Yesterday's Communal Offerings for Next Year

micro_segments.title: The Shekalim Case: When Money Becomes Registered as Paid (After Trumah Is Taken)
If Lost Money Is Found After Trumah, It's Paid; If Before, the City Owes Again and Must Collect Twice
The Mishnah's Debate: Can You Use Duplicate Shekalim for Next Year's Obligation
Rabbi Yehudah's Principle: An Obligation for This Year Can Be Applied to Next Year If Not Used
Abaye's Challenge: Why Can't You Use Last Year's Communal Offerings (Ox, Goat, Sin Offerings) for This Year
Why Dies Rather Than Graze: The Rule That This Year's Offering Must Be Bought from This Year's New Funds

macro_segments.title: The Lottery Problem: Why Last Year's Designation Doesn't Transfer to This Year

micro_segments.title: Rav Zeira's Answer: The Lottery Only Applies to the Year It Was Drawn; Last Year's Lot Doesn't Bind This Year
Why Not Redraw the Lottery This Year: You Could Start Fresh, but a Gezerah Prevents It (People Will Think the Old Lot Carries Over)
The Ox Case: If the Kohen Gadol Dies, His Ox Cannot Be Used by His Successor (Different Owner)
Gezerah L'Gezerah: Allowing the Goat Leads to Allowing the Ox, Allowing the Ox Leads to Allowing the Goat
The Real Reason for the Goat: It Will Definitely Be More Than a Year Old and Disqualified
According to Rabbi: A Year Is Measured Solarly (365 Days), So It's Theoretically Possible the Goat Is Still Valid
Final Conclusion: The Goat Dies Because It's Almost Certainly Too Old; the Ox Dies Because the Owner Likely Died

topical_tags.term: Rashi's Teaching on Lottery as Core
